perturbation.original_text: 5. Neither party shall, with out the prior written con sent of the other party, copy or reproduce any document which may be supplied hereun der and either party receiving any such document will a) return the same and any copies made thereof to the party supplying the same if so requested by the supplying party and b) certify in writing that it has complied with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Neither party should copy or reproduce any document which may be supplied hereunder without discussing the matter with the other party. Either party receiving any such document should consider returning the same and any copies made thereof to the party supplying the same if reasonably requested by the supplying party and may provide a certification in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses 'shall' and 'will' creating a legal obligation to refrain from copying and to return documents if requested. It further obligates the receiving party to certify compliance. The modified text uses 'should,' 'discussing the matter' 'consider', and 'may provide', creating a discretionary action rather than a mandatory obligation. This weakens the protections afforded to the disclosing party and opens the door to disputes regarding the handling of confidential documents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Spanish Intellectual Property Law (Ley de Propiedad Intelectual, RDL 1/1996)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity that undermines the stringent protections of the Spanish Intellectual Property Law, specifically regarding unauthorized reproduction of documents (Article 27). While the NDA aims to safeguard confidential information, the weakening of the clause concerning copying and returning documents conflicts with the law's intent to strictly control reproduction, distribution and communication of an original work that is protected by copyright.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: or authors who wish to take advantage of it. Registration provides prima facie evidence of creation and authorship (art. 140.3, renumbered to art. 145.3 by Ley 5/1998). Rights of exploitation ( Derechos de explotación ) [ edit ] The author has the exclusive legal right to exploit the work in any way or form, subject to the legal limitations on the exclusivity, and notably the rights of reproduction, distribution, public communication and transformation (art. 17). The author may transfer any or all of these rights to another person, although such a transfer cannot prevent the author from producing a collection (partial or complete) of his/her works (art. 22). Any agreement to transfer rights of exploitation must be made in writing (art. 45): it cannot cover the totality of the future works of an author (art. 43.3), cannot require the author not to produce works in the future (art. 43.4) and cannot cover forms of distribution which do not exist at the time of the agreement (art. 43.5). Such an agreement must normally guarantee the author a reasonable share of the income derived from the exploitation of the work (art. 46.1, 47), although a fixed-sum payment is allowed in certain cases (art. 46.2). The different rights of exploitation are independent of one another (art. 23). The authors of works of plastic art have the right to 3% of the resale price of their works when the resale price is greater than or equal to 300,000 pesetas (1,807 euros ): this right cannot be renounced or transferred during the lifetime of the author (art. 24). Duration of the rights of exploitation [ edit ] The main dispositions concerning the duration of copyright are contained in Title III, Chapter 1, as modified. In general, copyright protection in Spain lasts for the life of the author plus seventy years (art. 26). Collective works are protected for seventy years following publication (art. 28.2), as are pseudonymous and anonymous works unless the identity of the author becomes known (art. 27.2). It always assigns copyright to the author and he or she is not allowed to disclaim it. Posthumous works are protected for seventy years following publication provided they are published within seventy years of the death of the author. All of these time periods are calculated starting from 1 January following death or publication (art. 30). In the case of authors that died before 7 December 1987,the exploitations rights last for the time granted by the 1879 law, which granted a term of 80 years, but it is not clear if the 1 January rules applies in this case [ 1 ] Copyright management societies [ edit ] As in other countries, there are a number of societies which collectively manage the licensing of different types of work and the collection of royalties on behalf of copyright holders. These societies ( entidades de gestión ) are governed by Title IV of Book Three of the Intellectual Property Law (arts. 142–152, renumbered as arts. 147–157 by the Ley 5/1998), which provides for their authorisation by the Ministry of Culture. The largest such copyright management society is the Sociedad General de Autores y Editores (SGAE) , which was established by law in 1941 as the Sociedad General de Autores de España [4] and which had a de facto monopoly on the collective management of copyright royalties before the passage of the 1987 Intellectual Property Law. Moral rights ( Derecho moral ) [ edit ] The moral rights of the author, contained in Chapter 3, Section 1, of Title II, go beyond the minimum requirements of the Berne Convention . They are enumerated in article 14: the right to decide whether the work should be published and in what form the right to publish the work under his/her real name, pseudonymously or anonymously the right to be identified as the author of the work the right to insist on the respect of the integrity of the work and to prevent any distortion, modification, alteration or derogatory action in relation to the work which would be prejudicial to h
perturbation.justification: The original contract establishes a clear obligation regarding the handling of confidential documents, using 'shall' and 'will' to mandate actions such as refraining from copying and returning documents upon request. The changed text weakens these obligations by using terms like 'should,' 'discussing the matter,' 'consider,' and 'may provide,' which introduce discretion and reduce the enforceability of the clause. The Law Explanation suggests that this undermines intellectual property law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 touches upon rights of exploitation, stating, 'The author has the exclusive legal right to exploit the work in any way or form, subject to the legal limitations on the exclusivity, and notably the rights of reproduction, distribution, public communication and transformation (art. 17).' While this snippet doesn't directly address the specific wording changes, it highlights the broader intent to protect intellectual property, which the modified contract language weakens, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The obligations of confidentiality in this agreemen t shall cease when both parties give written notification of its termination, or 5 years after the date hereof, whichever is the sooner.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The confidentiality obligations in this agreement may be reviewed and possibly cease when both parties provide some form of communication regarding its potential termination, or approximately 5 years after the date hereof, potentially sooner.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear termination condition: written notification or 5 years. The modified text replaces 'shall cease' with 'may be reviewed and possibly cease', 'written notification' with 'some form of communication regarding its potential termination' and '5 years after the date hereof' with 'approximately 5 years after the date hereof, potentially sooner'. The effect of these changes is to make the termination of the confidentiality obligations highly uncertain and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1256 of the Spanish Civil Code states that the validity and fulfillment of contracts cannot be left to the discretion of only one of the contracting parties. By making the termination of confidentiality obligations uncertain and dependent on 'some form of communication' and 'approximately 5 years,' the modified clause creates a situation where one party could potentially unilaterally extend or shorten the duration of the agreement, contradicting the principles of contractual certainty and mutual agreement under Spanish law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity concerning the termination of confidentiality obligations, replacing clear terms with vague alternatives. The original text requires 'written notification' for termination, whereas the changed text allows for 'some form of communication regarding its potential termination.' Similarly, the clear timeline of '5 years' is replaced with 'approximately 5 years, potentially sooner.' This ambiguity contradicts Article 1256 of the Spanish Civil Code (as mentioned in the Law Explanation), which states that the validity and fulfillment of contracts cannot be left to the discretion of only one party. By using imprecise language, the modified text opens the door for unilateral interpretation and potential manipulation of the agreement's duration, creating a contradiction under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This agreement must be in accordance with, and gove rned in all respects by the laws of Spain.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This agreement should generally be interpreted in light of the spirit of the laws of Spain.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the contract is governed 'in all respects' by the laws of Spain. The changed text weakens this by stating the agreement 'should generally be interpreted in light of the spirit of the laws of Spain.' This phrasing replaces a strict legal mandate with a suggestion, opening the door to interpretations that may not fully comply with Spanish law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1091 of the Spanish Civil Code establishes that contracts have the force of law between the contracting parties and must be fulfilled according to their terms. The original text adheres to this principle by stating the agreement is governed 'in all respects' by Spanish law. The altered clause, which suggests interpreting the agreement in light of the 'spirit' of Spanish law, allows for subjective interpretations that may deviate from the actual legal requirements. This contradicts the obligatory nature of contracts as outlined in Article 1091.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'must be in accordance with, and governed in all respects by' to 'should generally be interpreted in light of the spirit of the laws' significantly weakens the contract's adherence to Spanish law. The original text creates a direct and binding obligation, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and allows for subjective interpretation. Article 1091 of the Spanish Civil Code, as described in the Law Explanation, states that contracts 'have the force of law between the contracting parties and must be fulfilled according to their terms.' The modified language undermines this principle by suggesting a looser interpretation based on the 'spirit' rather than the precise requirements of Spanish law, creating an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, as defined in the instructions.
